evidence_quotes.quote: EULA reflects a licensing agreement rather than the sale of an actual product.
remove legally acquired copies from customers' digital libraries entirely
Language like purchase ACCESS to XYZ game or LICENSE XYZ game would prevent customers from being purposefully misled by these companies.
This happened with Ubisofts The Crew, which was removed from digital storefronts, followed by shutting down the online servers, rendering the game unplayable.

paragraphs.text: Dear legislators, I humbly request to consider extending the Digital Fairness Act to cover digital ownership, specifically regarding video games. A far too common malpractice in digital spaces is the erosion of digital ownership. Companies sell licenses to products using language such as buy XYZ product but then remove ownership when the product is no longer profitable for them. A concrete example in the video game industry, where companies like Ubisoft, Activision Blizzard, Microsoft, Electronic Arts, Epic Games, and many more sell video games on digital storefronts with misleading advertising, claiming to sell a product, yet their EULA reflects a licensing agreement rather than the sale of an actual product. This has led customers to buy games, such as Ubisofts The Crew, falsely believing they own them after being misled on said storefront. However, this is false advertising at best, since its not the sale of a product, rather a licensing agreement which can be revoked at will by the company. This happened with Ubisofts The Crew, which was removed from digital storefronts, followed by shutting down the online servers, rendering the game unplayable. Furthermore, these companies are not legally obligated to provide end-of-life services for these games, leaving customers with no access to already purchased games. Ubisoft even went as far as to remove legally acquired copies from customers' digital libraries entirely, preventing downloads, installations, and gameplay AFTER purchase, with no compensation to customers, solely to save money. This at best, sounds like a scam. My request isn't to force these companies into providing perpetual online services. Rather, I humbly request to require them to offer end-of-life services allowing customers continued access to products they purchased after official support ends. Such services could range from implementing peer-to-peer server models to renting server capacities. Contrary to what these companies would like you to believe, end-of-life services are feasible and have been implemented in the past. Electronic Arts Battlefield 4" (2013), remains purchasable and playable thanks to rented server capacity officially offered by EA, illustrating such end-of-life services are feasible. Why this isn't offered for other EA games like the recently shut-down Anthem is mystery to me as a customer. Other games allow peer-to-peer connectivity, enabling a private individual who purchased the game to host servers on personal hardware. However, such services are increasingly uncommon, presumably to cut costs or exploit customer bases financially. Take Two & 2K's basketball video game series NBA 2K exemplifies this issue. A new game in the series releases annually, resulting in older entries' servers shutting down, leaving the product unusable years after purchase with no end-of-life services. Customers are then forced to repurchase essentially identical products regularly, because they are losing access to already legally purchased content. Additionally, I humbly request to enforce transparency about the type of purchase made. Instead of allowing companies to mislead consumers about ownership with terms like sell or purchase, companies should clearly be required to state the licensing nature of these transactions. Language like purchase ACCESS to XYZ game or LICENSE XYZ game would prevent customers from being purposefully misled by these companies. These are just a few examples of widespread malpractices and anti-consumer behaviors within the video games industry, including gambling-like loot boxes targeting children and outright deleting products after purchase. Companies regularly abuse their power, blatantly harming their customers for profits. Without regulatory intervention, these businesses will continue to abuse their power and continue such detrimental practices. Thank you for taking this issue into consideration.
